feed.title: SAP Community - SAP HANA Cloud
feed.subtitle: SAP HANA Cloud blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: Initializing Replication from SAP ASE to SAP HANA Cloud HANA using Bulk Materialization
entry.content:
This article provides step-by-step instructions for setting up a real-time replication environment from SAP Adaptive Server Enterprise (ASE) On Premise to SAP HANA Cloud HANA database (HANA) using a BTP subaccount and SAP Replication Server (SRS).

Table level replication is used to replicate required tables.  Using table level replication allows finer control over database replication as not all tables may need to be replicated.

 

1. Install, create and configure an ASE On Premise

ASE is installed in the $SYBASE folder (Adaptive Server Enterprise/16.1 SP00 PL00/EBF 30672 SMP/P/x86_64/Linux 5.14.21-150400.24.147-default/ase161sp00pl00/3343/64-bit/FBO/Thu Feb 6 02:47:09 2025).  Instance SAMPLE_ASE is running on port 5000.

An empty 'tpcc' database (~2GB) will be used as the starting point on the ASE.

 

2. Download and install SAP HANA Client

The SAP HANA Client can be downloaded from https://tools.hana.ondemand.com for the platform on which SRS will be running (for this blog, the client used is 2.24.24).

Unpack and install to an hdbclient directory (e.g. /opt/sap/hdbclient).

Add the HANA client directory to the PATH and ensure that it will be persisted when the future SRS instance created is started. e.g. add the following to ~/.bashrc :

export PATH=<HANA Client location>:$PATH

 SRS must be able to access the HANA ODBC driver.

 

3. Install, create and configure an SRS On Premise.

SRS is installed in its own $SYBASE path (Replication Server/16.1/EBF 30761 SP00 PL00 rs161sp00pl00/Linux AMD64/Linux 5.14.21-150400.24.136-default x86_64/3251/OPT64/Sun Dec 29 10:05:13 2024).  A SAMPLE_RS instance is created using $SYBASE/$SYBASE_REP/install/rs_init and running on port 11752.

For this example, the replication server's 'memory_limit' is set to 16 GB and the partition size is 2 GB.

Run the following commands on the replication server to finish configuration:

 

configure replication server set dsi_bulk_copy to "off"
configure replication server set dynamic_sql to "off"
configure replication server set memory_limit to "16384"

 

4. Create symlink for libodbcHDB.so

Create a symlink in the $SYBASE/$SYBASE_REP/connector/lib for libodbcHDB.so:

ln -s <HANA Client location>/libodbcHDB.so $SYBASE/$SYBASE_REP/connector/lib/libodbcHDB.so

alternatively, a copy of the library can be placed in the location:

cp <HANA Client location>/libodbcHDB.so $SYBASE/$SYBASE_REP/connector/lib/.

 

5. Create echsslattr.txt

Create a directory under $SYBASE (e.g. $SYBASE/secudir).  e.g:

mkdir $SYBASE/secudir

Create a file named echsslattr.txt in that directory with the single line 'encrypt=true;'. e.g.:

echo 'encrypt=true;' > $SYBASE/secudir/echsslattr.txt

 

6. Set additional environment variables

Set the following environment variables:

export USE_SSL_ECH_ATTR=1
export PATH_SSL_ECH_ATTR=$SYBASE/secudir # folder from step 4

Note: It is suggested to add this to the same script (e.g. ~/.bashrc) where the PATH is set to find the HANA Client installation.

 

7. Restart the SRS server

Shutdown and restart the SRS server instance to enable the environment variables to be picked up by SRS.  (This may require running the necessary script(s), exit and re-login, etc).

It is critical that the SRS server can access these environment variables.  libodbcHDB.so will be found in LD_LIBRARY_PATH (when set by SYBASE.sh), the rest of the client and configuration are found by the SRS instance using the environment variables set before instance startup.

 

8. Create HANA instance in BTP

Connect to SAP BTP Cockpit (e.g. https://amer.cockpit.btp.cloud.sap) and access the sub account from the 'Overview' where the HANA instance will be created. 

SAP HANA Cloud Central must be used to create the HANA instance.  From BTP Cockpit, install (if necessary) 'SAP HANA Cloud' application from the 'tools' plan and 'Go to Application'.

Once connected and running SAP HANA Cloud Central, create a new HANA instance of type 'SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database'.

  • Instance Name: HANA
  • Set and confirm DBADMIN password
  • Memory: 32 GB
  • Storage: 120 GB
  • Allowed connections: Allow all IP addresses
  • All other settings can remain as defaults.

Press 'Review and Create'.

Once created, copy the SQL Endpoint for this instance to the clipboard using the '...' (Actions) -> 'Copy SQL Endpoint' menu for the instance:

This endpoint contains the FQDN and port (usually 443) of the instance.  This information is used in the next step.

 

9.  Add the HANA and ASE endpoints to the SRS interfaces file.

Edit the $SYBASE/interfaces file for the SRS instance and add details for connections to the ASE and HANA.  Linux example:

SAMPLE_RS
   master tcp ether <SRS instance host> 11752
   query tcp ether <SRS instance host> 11752

SAMPLE_RS_ERSSD
   master tcp ether localhost 11751
   query tcp ether localhost 11751

SAMPLE_ASE
   master tcp ether <ASE instance host> 5000
   query tcp ether <ASE instance host> 5000

HANA
   master tcp ether <FQDN from SQL Endpoint> 443
   query tcp ether <FQDN from SQL Endpoint> 443

(The SSL filter, ssl="CN=hanacloud.ondemand.com" will not be used as the actual connection will be via the HANA ODBC driver).

 

10. Add the SRS endpoint to the ASE interfaces file.

Edit the $SYBASE/interfaces file for the ASE instance and add details for connections to the SRS. 

Example:

SAMPLE_ASE
   master tcp ether <ASE instance host> 5000
   query tcp ether <ASE instance host> 5000

SAMPLE_ASE_BS
   master tcp ether <ASE instance host> 5001
   query tcp ether <ASE instance host> 5001

SAMPLE_ASE_JSAGENT
   master tcp ether <ASE instance host> 4900
   query tcp ether <ASE instance host> 4900

SAMPLE_ASE_XP
   master tcp ether <ASE instance host>5002
   query tcp ether <ASE instance host>5002

SAMPLE_RS
   master tcp ether <SRS instance host> 11752
   query tcp ether <SRS instance host> 11752

 

11. Create the database and tables in ASE.

Connect to the ASE database using isql or DBISQL.  After creating the database, run the script below in the database to create the tables, columns, indexes and RI contraints:

set quoted_identifier on
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: CUSTOMER                                              */
/*==============================================================*/
create table CUSTOMER (
   C_ID                 int            default '0' not null,
   C_D_ID               smallint       default '0' not null,
   C_W_ID               smallint       default '0' not null,
   C_FIRST              varchar(16)                    null,
   C_MIDDLE             char(2)                        null,
   C_LAST               varchar(16)                    null,
   C_STREET_1           varchar(20)                    null,
   C_STREET_2           varchar(20)                    null,
   C_CITY               varchar(20)                    null,
   C_STATE              char(2)                        null,
   C_ZIP                char(9)                        null,
   C_PHONE              char(16)                       null,
   C_SINCE              DATETIME     default getdate() null,
   C_CREDIT             char(2)                        null,
   C_CREDIT_LIM         decimal(12,2)                  null,
   C_DISCOUNT           decimal(4,4)                   null,
   C_BALANCE            decimal(12,2)                  null,
   C_YTD_PAYMENT        decimal(12,2)                  null,
   C_PAYMENT_CNT        int                            null,
   C_DELIVERY_CNT       int                            null,
   C_DATA               varchar(500)                   null,
   constraint C_PRIMARY primary key (C_W_ID, C_D_ID, C_ID)
)
lock datarows
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: C_C_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index C_C_ID on CUSTOMER (
C_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: C_D_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index C_D_ID on CUSTOMER (
C_D_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: C_W_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index C_W_ID on CUSTOMER (
C_W_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: DISTRICT                                              */
/*==============================================================*/
create table DISTRICT (
   D_ID                 smallint       default '0' not null,
   D_W_ID               smallint       default '0' not null,
   D_NAME               varchar(10)                    null,
   D_STREET_1           varchar(20)                    null,
   D_STREET_2           varchar(20)                    null,
   D_CITY               varchar(20)                    null,
   D_STATE              char(2)                        null,
   D_ZIP                char(9)                        null,
   D_TAX                decimal(4,4)                   null,
   D_YTD                decimal(12,2)                  null,
   D_NEXT_O_ID          int                            null,
   constraint D_PRIMARY primary key (D_W_ID, D_ID)
)
lock datarows
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: D_D_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index D_D_ID on DISTRICT (
D_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: D_W_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index D_W_ID on DISTRICT (
D_W_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: HISTORY                                               */
/*==============================================================*/
create table HISTORY (
   H_C_ID               int                            null,
   H_C_D_ID             smallint                       null,
   H_C_W_ID             smallint                       null,
   H_D_ID               smallint                       null,
   H_W_ID               smallint           default '0' null,
   H_DATE               DATETIME     default getdate() null,
   H_AMOUNT             decimal(6,2)                   null,
   H_DATA               varchar(24)                    null
)
lock datarows
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: ITEM                                                  */
/*==============================================================*/
create table ITEM (
   I_ID                 int            default '0' not null,
   I_IM_ID              int                            null,
   I_NAME               varchar(24)                    null,
   I_PRICE              decimal(5,2)                   null,
   I_DATA               varchar(50)                    null,
   constraint PK_ITEM primary key (I_ID)
)
lock datarows
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: NEW_ORDER                                             */
/*==============================================================*/
create table NEW_ORDER (
   NO_O_ID              int            default '0' not null,
   NO_D_ID              smallint       default '0' not null,
   NO_W_ID              smallint       default '0' not null,
   constraint NO_PRIMARY primary key (NO_W_ID, NO_D_ID, NO_O_ID)
)
lock datarows
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: ORDER                                                 */
/*==============================================================*/
create table "ORDER" (
   O_ID                 int            default '0' not null,
   O_D_ID               smallint       default '0' not null,
   O_W_ID               smallint       default '0' not null,
   O_C_ID               int                            null,
   O_ENTRY_D            DATETIME     default getdate() null,
   O_CARRIER_ID         int                            null,
   O_OL_CNT             smallint                       null,
   O_ALL_LOCAL          smallint                       null,
   constraint O_PRIMARY primary key (O_W_ID, O_D_ID, O_ID)
)
lock datarows
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: O_O_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index O_O_ID on "ORDER" (
O_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: O_D_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index O_D_ID on "ORDER" (
O_D_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: O_W_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index O_W_ID on "ORDER" (
O_W_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: O_C_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index O_C_ID on "ORDER" (
O_C_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: ORDER_LINE                                            */
/*==============================================================*/
create table ORDER_LINE (
   OL_O_ID              int            default '0' not null,
   OL_D_ID              smallint       default '0' not null,
   OL_W_ID              smallint       default '0' not null,
   OL_NUMBER            int            default '0' not null,
   OL_I_ID              int                            null,
   OL_SUPPLY_W_ID       smallint                       null,
   OL_DELIVERY_D        DATETIME                       null,
   OL_QUANTITY          smallint                            null,
   OL_AMOUNT            decimal(6,2)                   null,
   OL_DIST_INFO         char(24)                       null,
   constraint OL_PRIMARY primary key (OL_W_ID, OL_D_ID, OL_O_ID, OL_NUMBER)
)
lock datarows
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: IDX1                                                  */
/*==============================================================*/
create index IDX1 on ORDER_LINE (
OL_W_ID ASC,
OL_I_ID ASC,
OL_D_ID ASC,
OL_O_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: IDX2                                                  */
/*==============================================================*/
create index IDX2 on ORDER_LINE (
OL_O_ID ASC,
OL_D_ID ASC,
OL_W_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: OL_O_ID                                               */
/*==============================================================*/
create index OL_O_ID on ORDER_LINE (
OL_O_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: OL_D_ID                                               */
/*==============================================================*/
create index OL_D_ID on ORDER_LINE (
OL_D_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: OL_W_ID                                               */
/*==============================================================*/
create index OL_W_ID on ORDER_LINE (
OL_W_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: OL_I_ID                                               */
/*==============================================================*/
create index OL_I_ID on ORDER_LINE (
OL_I_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: OL_SUPPLY_W_ID                                        */
/*==============================================================*/
create index OL_SUPPLY_W_ID on ORDER_LINE (
OL_SUPPLY_W_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: STOCK                                                 */
/*==============================================================*/
create table STOCK (
   S_I_ID               int            default '0' not null,
   S_W_ID               smallint       default '0' not null,
   S_QUANTITY           int            default '0' not null,
   S_DIST_01            char(24)                       null,
   S_DIST_02            char(24)                       null,
   S_DIST_03            char(24)                       null,
   S_DIST_04            char(24)                       null,
   S_DIST_05            char(24)                       null,
   S_DIST_06            char(24)                       null,
   S_DIST_07            char(24)                       null,
   S_DIST_08            char(24)                       null,
   S_DIST_09            char(24)                       null,
   S_DIST_10            char(24)                       null,
   S_YTD                int                            null,
   S_ORDER_CNT          int                            null,
   S_REMOTE_CNT         int                            null,
   S_DATA               varchar(50)                    null,
   constraint S_PRIMARY primary key (S_W_ID, S_I_ID)
)
lock datarows
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: S_I_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index S_I_ID on STOCK (
S_I_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: S_W_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index S_W_ID on STOCK (
S_W_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: WAREHOUSE                                             */
/*==============================================================*/
create table WAREHOUSE (
   W_ID                 smallint       default '0' not null,
   W_NAME               varchar(10)                    null,
   W_STREET_1           varchar(20)                    null,
   W_STREET_2           varchar(20)                    null,
   W_CITY               varchar(20)                    null,
   W_STATE              char(2)                        null,
   W_ZIP                char(9)                        null,
   W_TAX                decimal(4,4)                   null,
   W_YTD                decimal(12,2)                  null,
   constraint W_PRIMARY primary key (W_ID)
)
lock datarows
go

alter table CUSTOMER
   add constraint C_FKEY_D foreign key (C_W_ID, C_D_ID)
      references DISTRICT (D_W_ID, D_ID)
go

alter table DISTRICT
   add constraint D_FKEY_W foreign key (D_W_ID)
      references WAREHOUSE (W_ID)
go

alter table HISTORY
   add constraint H_FKEY_C foreign key (H_C_W_ID, H_C_D_ID, H_C_ID)
      references CUSTOMER (C_W_ID, C_D_ID, C_ID)
go

alter table HISTORY
   add constraint H_FKEY_D foreign key (H_W_ID, H_D_ID)
      references DISTRICT (D_W_ID, D_ID)
go

alter table NEW_ORDER
   add constraint NO_FKEY_O foreign key (NO_W_ID, NO_D_ID, NO_O_ID)
      references "ORDER" (O_W_ID, O_D_ID, O_ID)
go

alter table "ORDER"
   add constraint O_FKEY_C foreign key (O_W_ID, O_D_ID, O_C_ID)
      references CUSTOMER (C_W_ID, C_D_ID, C_ID)
go

alter table ORDER_LINE
   add constraint OL_FKEY_O foreign key (OL_W_ID, OL_D_ID, OL_O_ID)
      references "ORDER" (O_W_ID, O_D_ID, O_ID)
go
alter table ORDER_LINE
   add constraint OL_FKEY_S foreign key (OL_SUPPLY_W_ID, OL_I_ID)
      references STOCK (S_W_ID, S_I_ID)
go

alter table STOCK
   add constraint S_FKEY_I foreign key (S_I_ID)
      references ITEM (I_ID)
go

alter table STOCK
   add constraint S_FKEY_W foreign key (S_W_ID)
      references WAREHOUSE (W_ID)
go

 

12. Create user and schema in HANA.

Connect to the HANA endpoint as DBADMIN using the Database Explorer or SQL Console from HANA Cloud Central and issue the following SQL to create the user and grant a privilege necessary for enabling HVAR (the first step of HVAR enablement):

create user tpcc password <password> no force_first_password_change;
grant create schema to tpcc;

 

13. Create synonym for TPCC schema

Connect to the HANA endpoint as tpcc using the Database Explorer or SQL Console from HANA Cloud Central and issue the following SQL (the second step of HVAR enablement):

create schema synonym dbo for tpcc;

 

14. Create the tables in HANA

Remaining connected to HANA as tpcc using the Database Explorer or SQL Console from HANA Cloud Central, issue the following SQL to create the tables (no RI is defined in this script to allow only specific tables to be replicated if desired):

CREATE COLUMN TABLE WAREHOUSE (
W_ID SMALLINT,
W_NAME VARCHAR(10),
W_STREET_1 VARCHAR(20),
W_STREET_2 VARCHAR(20),
W_CITY VARCHAR(20),
W_STATE CHAR(2),
W_ZIP CHAR(9),
W_TAX DECIMAL(4,4),
W_YTD DECIMAL(12,2),
PRIMARY KEY ("W_ID")
) PARTITION BY HASH (W_ID) PARTITIONS 5;

CREATE COLUMN TABLE DISTRICT (
D_ID SMALLINT,
D_W_ID SMALLINT,
D_NAME VARCHAR(10),
D_STREET_1 VARCHAR(20),
D_STREET_2 VARCHAR(20),
D_CITY VARCHAR(20),
D_STATE CHAR(2),
D_ZIP CHAR(9),
D_TAX DECIMAL(4,4),
D_YTD DECIMAL(12,2),
D_NEXT_O_ID INTEGER,
PRIMARY KEY ("D_W_ID", "D_ID")
) PARTITION BY HASH (D_W_ID) PARTITIONS 5;

CREATE COLUMN TABLE ITEM (
I_ID INTEGER,
I_IM_ID INTEGER,
I_NAME VARCHAR(24),
I_PRICE DECIMAL(5,2),
I_DATA VARCHAR(50),
PRIMARY KEY ("I_ID")
);

CREATE COLUMN TABLE CUSTOMER (
C_ID INTEGER,
C_D_ID SMALLINT,
C_W_ID SMALLINT,
C_FIRST VARCHAR(16),
C_MIDDLE CHAR(2),
C_LAST VARCHAR(16),
C_STREET_1 VARCHAR(20),
C_STREET_2 VARCHAR(20),
C_CITY VARCHAR(20),
C_STATE CHAR(2),
C_ZIP CHAR(9),
C_PHONE CHAR(16),
C_SINCE TIMESTAMP,
C_CREDIT CHAR(2),
C_CREDIT_LIM DECIMAL(12,2),
C_DISCOUNT DECIMAL(4,4),
C_BALANCE DECIMAL(12,2),
C_YTD_PAYMENT DECIMAL(12,2),
C_PAYMENT_CNT INTEGER,
C_DELIVERY_CNT INTEGER,
C_DATA VARCHAR(500),
PRIMARY KEY ("C_W_ID", "C_D_ID", "C_ID")
) PARTITION BY HASH (C_W_ID) PARTITIONS 5;

CREATE COLUMN TABLE HISTORY (
H_C_ID INTEGER,
H_C_D_ID SMALLINT,
H_C_W_ID SMALLINT,
H_D_ID SMALLINT,
H_W_ID SMALLINT,
H_DATE TIMESTAMP,
H_AMOUNT DECIMAL(6,2),
H_DATA VARCHAR(24)
);

CREATE TABLE STOCK (
S_I_ID INTEGER,
S_W_ID SMALLINT,
S_QUANTITY INTEGER,
S_DIST_01 CHAR(24),
S_DIST_02 CHAR(24),
S_DIST_03 CHAR(24),
S_DIST_04 CHAR(24),
S_DIST_05 CHAR(24),
S_DIST_06 CHAR(24),
S_DIST_07 CHAR(24),
S_DIST_08 CHAR(24),
S_DIST_09 CHAR(24),
S_DIST_10 CHAR(24),
S_YTD INTEGER,
S_ORDER_CNT INTEGER,
S_REMOTE_CNT INTEGER,
S_DATA VARCHAR(50),
PRIMARY KEY ("S_W_ID", "S_I_ID")
) PARTITION BY HASH (S_W_ID) PARTITIONS 5;

CREATE TABLE "ORDER" (
O_ID INTEGER,
O_D_ID SMALLINT,
O_W_ID SMALLINT,
O_C_ID INTEGER,
O_ENTRY_D TIMESTAMP,
O_CARRIER_ID INTEGER,
O_OL_CNT INTEGER,
O_ALL_LOCAL INTEGER,
PRIMARY KEY ("O_W_ID", "O_D_ID", "O_ID")
) PARTITION BY HASH (O_W_ID) PARTITIONS 5;

CREATE TABLE NEW_ORDER (
NO_O_ID INTEGER,
NO_D_ID SMALLINT,
NO_W_ID SMALLINT,
PRIMARY KEY ("NO_W_ID", "NO_D_ID", "NO_O_ID")
) PARTITION BY HASH (NO_W_ID) PARTITIONS 5;

CREATE TABLE ORDER_LINE (
OL_O_ID INTEGER,
OL_D_ID SMALLINT,
OL_W_ID SMALLINT,
OL_NUMBER INTEGER,
OL_I_ID INTEGER,
OL_SUPPLY_W_ID SMALLINT,
OL_DELIVERY_D TIMESTAMP,
OL_QUANTITY INTEGER,
OL_AMOUNT DECIMAL(6,2),
OL_DIST_INFO VARCHAR(24),
PRIMARY KEY ("OL_W_ID", "OL_D_ID", "OL_O_ID", "OL_NUMBER")
) PARTITION BY HASH (OL_W_ID) PARTITIONS 5;

 

15. Create connection from SRS to ASE

As ASE holds the source database, the connection must be created using rs_init.  The rs_init resource file to use will be:

sybinit.release_directory: <$SYBASE from SRS installation>
sybinit.product: rs
rs.rs_operation: rs_setup_db
#
rs.rs_name: SAMPLE_RS
rs.rs_rs_sa_user: sa
rs.rs_rs_sa_pass: <SRS sa password>
#
rs.rs_ds_name: SAMPLE_ASE

# sa login for this Adaptive Server
rs.rs_ds_sa_user: sa
rs.rs_ds_sa_password: <ASE sa password>

# name of the database that you are adding to the replicated data system
rs.rs_db_name: tpcc
rs.rs_needs_repagent: yes
# name of the user who will update replicated data
# default <rs_db_name>_maint
# rs.rs_db_maint_user: USE_DEFAULT
rs.rs_db_maint_user: tpcc_maint
rs.rs_db_maint_password: <create a pwd>

# rep agent login
rs.rs_ltm_rs_user: SAMPLE_RS_ra
rs.rs_ltm_rs_pass: <rs.rs_ltm_rs_pass from SAMPLE_RS rs_init creation resource file>

 

Create the resource file on the SRS server and submit to the SRS instance using the SRS host shell:

$SYBASE/$SYBASE_REP/install/rs_init -r <resource file>

 

16. Configure the ASE replication agent  

Connect to the ASE instance with isql/DBISQL and issue the following:

use tpcc
go
exec sp_reptostandby tpcc, 'all'
go
exec sp_config_rep_agent tpcc,send_warm_standby_xacts,true
go
exec sp_stop_rep_agent tpcc
go
exec sp_start_rep_agent tpcc
go

 

17. Mark tables for replication

exec sp_setreptable ITEM, 'true', owner_on
exec sp_setreptable WAREHOUSE, 'true', owner_on
exec sp_setreptable DISTRICT, 'true', owner_on
exec sp_setreptable STOCK, 'true', owner_on
exec sp_setreptable CUSTOMER, 'true', owner_on
exec sp_setreptable 'ORDER', 'true', owner_on
exec sp_setreptable HISTORY, 'true', owner_on
exec sp_setreptable ORDER_LINE, 'true', owner_on
exec sp_setreptable NEW_ORDER, 'true', owner_on
go

 

18. Create connection from SRS to HANA

Connect to SAMPLE_RS using isql/DBISQL and issue the following RCL:

create connection to HANA.HANA using profile rs_ase_to_hanadb;ech
set username to TPCC 
set password to "<password>"
set dsi_quoted_identifier to "always"
set dsi_compile_enable to "on"
set ddl_dup_detect to "off"
set dsi_compile_retry_threshold to "0"
go

The connection is created (confirm using 'admin who').  Once created, run the following SQL to complete HVAR enablement of the connection (this is the 3rd and final step of HVAR enablement):

alter connection to HANA.HANA
set schemamap from SAMPLE_ASE.tpcc.dbo to "tpcc"
go

Then suspend and resume the connection:

suspend connection to HANA.HANA
go
resume connection to HANA.HANA
go

 

19. Create table replication definitions

Connect to the SRS and issue the following RCL commands to create the table replication definitions:

create replication definition ITEM_rep 
with primary at SAMPLE_ASE.tpcc
with primary table named dbo.'ITEM'
with replicate table named 'ITEM'
(
	I_ID    int,
	I_IM_ID int,
	I_NAME  varchar(24),
	I_PRICE decimal,
	I_DATA  varchar(50)
)
primary key (I_ID)
go
create replication definition WAREHOUSE_rep 
with primary at SAMPLE_ASE.tpcc
with primary table named dbo.'WAREHOUSE'
with replicate table named 'WAREHOUSE'
(
  W_ID int, 
  W_NAME     varchar(10),
  W_STREET_1 varchar(20),
  W_STREET_2 varchar(20),
  W_CITY     varchar(20),
  W_STATE    char(2),
  W_ZIP      char(9),
  W_TAX      decimal,
  W_YTD      decimal
)
primary key (W_ID)
go
create replication definition DISTRICT_rep 
with primary at SAMPLE_ASE.tpcc
with primary table named dbo.'DISTRICT'
with replicate table named 'DISTRICT'
(
   D_ID        int,
   D_W_ID      int,
   D_NAME      varchar(10),
   D_STREET_1  varchar(20),
   D_STREET_2  varchar(20),
   D_CITY      varchar(20),
   D_STATE     char(2),
   D_ZIP       char(9),
   D_TAX       decimal,
   D_YTD       decimal,
   D_NEXT_O_ID int
)
primary key (D_W_ID, D_ID)
go

create replication definition STOCK_rep with 
primary at SAMPLE_ASE.tpcc
with primary table named dbo.'STOCK' 
with replicate table named 'STOCK'
(
   S_I_ID       int,
   S_W_ID       int,
   S_QUANTITY   int,
   S_DIST_01    char(24),
   S_DIST_02    char(24),
   S_DIST_03    char(24),
   S_DIST_04    char(24),
   S_DIST_05    char(24),
   S_DIST_06    char(24),
   S_DIST_07    char(24),
   S_DIST_08    char(24),
   S_DIST_09    char(24),
   S_DIST_10    char(24),
   S_YTD        int,
   S_ORDER_CNT  int,
   S_REMOTE_CNT int,
   S_DATA       varchar(50)
 )
primary key (S_W_ID, S_I_ID)
go

create replication definition CUSTOMER_rep 
with primary at SAMPLE_ASE.tpcc
with primary table named dbo.'CUSTOMER'
with replicate table named 'CUSTOMER'
(
   C_ID           int,
   C_D_ID         int,
   C_W_ID         int,
   C_FIRST        varchar(16),
   C_MIDDLE       char(2),
   C_LAST         varchar(16),
   C_STREET_1     varchar(20),
   C_STREET_2     varchar(20),
   C_CITY         varchar(20),
   C_STATE        char(2),
   C_ZIP          char(9),
   C_PHONE        char(16),
   C_SINCE        datetime,
   C_CREDIT       char(2),
   C_CREDIT_LIM   decimal,
   C_DISCOUNT     decimal,
   C_BALANCE      decimal,
   C_YTD_PAYMENT  decimal,
   C_PAYMENT_CNT  integer,
   C_DELIVERY_CNT integer,
   C_DATA         varchar(500)
)
primary key (C_W_ID, C_D_ID, C_ID)
go

create replication definition ORDER_rep 
with primary at SAMPLE_ASE.tpcc
with primary table named dbo.'ORDER' 
with replicate table named 'ORDER' quoted
(
   O_ID         int,
   O_D_ID       int,
   O_W_ID       int,
   O_C_ID       int,
   O_ENTRY_D    datetime,
   O_CARRIER_ID int,
   O_OL_CNT     int,
   O_ALL_LOCAL  int
)
primary key (O_W_ID, O_D_ID, O_ID)
go

create replication definition HISTORY_rep 
with primary at SAMPLE_ASE.tpcc
with primary table named dbo.'HISTORY' 
with replicate table named 'HISTORY'
(
   H_C_ID   int,
   H_C_D_ID int,
   H_C_W_ID int,
   H_D_ID   int,
   H_W_ID   int,
   H_DATE   datetime,
   H_AMOUNT decimal,
   H_DATA   varchar(24)
)
primary key (H_C_ID, H_C_D_ID, H_W_ID, H_D_ID, H_W_ID)
go

create replication definition ORDER_LINE_rep 
with primary at SAMPLE_ASE.tpcc
with primary table named dbo.'ORDER_LINE' 
with replicate table named 'ORDER_LINE'
(
   OL_O_ID        int,
   OL_D_ID        int,
   OL_W_ID        int,
   OL_NUMBER      int,
   OL_I_ID        int,
   OL_SUPPLY_W_ID int,
   OL_DELIVERY_D  datetime,
   OL_QUANTITY    int,
   OL_AMOUNT      decimal,
   OL_DIST_INFO   char(24)
)
primary key (OL_W_ID, OL_D_ID, OL_O_ID, OL_NUMBER)
go

create replication definition NEW_ORDER_rep 
with primary at SAMPLE_ASE.tpcc
with primary table named dbo.'NEW_ORDER'
with replicate table named 'NEW_ORDER'
(
   NO_O_ID int,
   NO_D_ID int,
   NO_W_ID int
)
primary key (NO_W_ID, NO_D_ID, NO_O_ID)
go

 

20. Load data into ASE database

In this example, the application used to load the data performs the loading of data using java.sql.PreparedStatement.executeBatch() and java.sql.PreparedStatement.executeUpdate().

After loading the ASE database contains the following record counts:

 tablename            records
 -------------------- --------------------
 ITEM                               100000
 DISTRICT                               10
 WAREHOUSE                               1
 STOCK                              100000
 CUSTOMER                            30000
 ORDER                               30000
 ORDER_LINE                         300271
 HISTORY                             30000
 NEW_ORDER                            9000

 

21. Create and validate the table subscriptions for HANA

Subscription creation:

create subscription HANA_ITEM_sub for ITEM_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
without holdlock 
direct_load
user tpcc password <password>
subscribe to truncate table
go

create subscription HANA_WAREHOUSE_sub for WAREHOUSE_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
without holdlock 
direct_load
user tpcc password <password>
subscribe to truncate table
go

create subscription HANA_DISTRICT_sub for DISTRICT_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
without holdlock 
direct_load
user tpcc password <password>
subscribe to truncate table
go

create subscription HANA_STOCK_sub for STOCK_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
without holdlock 
direct_load
user tpcc password <password>
subscribe to truncate table
go

create subscription HANA_CUSTOMER_sub for CUSTOMER_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
without holdlock 
direct_load
user tpcc password <password>
subscribe to truncate table
go

create subscription HANA_ORDER_sub for ORDER_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
without holdlock 
direct_load
user tpcc password <password>
subscribe to truncate table
go

create subscription HANA_HISTORY_sub for HISTORY_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
without holdlock 
direct_load
user tpcc password <password>
subscribe to truncate table
go

create subscription HANA_ORDER_LINE_sub for ORDER_LINE_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
without holdlock 
direct_load
user tpcc password <password>
subscribe to truncate table
go

create subscription HANA_NEW_ORDER_sub for NEW_ORDER_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
without holdlock 
direct_load
user tpcc password <password>
subscribe to truncate table
go

A separate subscription thread is spawned in the SRS server for each table.  Setting a larger memory configuration in Step 3 allows the subscriptions to materialize in parallel.

Each subscription can be validated as follows:

check subscription HANA_ITEM_sub for ITEM_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
go
check subscription HANA_WAREHOUSE_sub for WAREHOUSE_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
go
check subscription HANA_DISTRICT_sub for DISTRICT_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
go
check subscription HANA_STOCK_sub for STOCK_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
go
check subscription HANA_CUSTOMER_sub for CUSTOMER_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
go
check subscription HANA_ORDER_sub for ORDER_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
go
check subscription HANA_HISTORY_sub for HISTORY_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
go
check subscription HANA_ORDER_LINE_sub for ORDER_LINE_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
go
check subscription HANA_NEW_ORDER_sub for NEW_ORDER_rep
with replicate at HANA.HANA
go

While a subscription is being materialized, an additional line of output showing the progress of materialization will be output when checked.  e.g.:

Subscription HANA_ORDER_LINE_sub is ACTIVE at the replicate.
Subscription HANA_ORDER_LINE_sub is ACTIVE at the primary.
Subscriptions HANA_ORDER_LINE_sub progress: initial loading, 94% done, 15923 commands remaining.

When materialization is complete, the 'SUB' thread will not show in 'admin who' output and the additional progress output will be removed from the 'check subscription' output. 

The record counts in HANA will match:

ITEM         100000
DISTRICT         10
WAREHOUSE         1
STOCK        100000
CUSTOMER      30000
ORDER         30000
ORDER_LINE   300271
HISTORY       30000
NEW_ORDER      9000

 

22. Run transactional load on ASE database

Transactions can be committed to the ASE database while materialization is occurring.  There is no need to suspend DML operations during materialization.

Replication will continue as more transactions are applied at the source database.

 

Finally

That's it!

Thanks to Mark Mumy, Dan Van Leeuwen, David Brandow,  Zhang Min, Gary Zhong and many others for their work on contributing to this blog.

entry.title: Build, Configure, and Deploy SAP CAP Projects to the Cloud
entry.content:
🚀A Comprehensive, Step-by-Step Guide to Creating and Deploying a Full CAP Application with SAP Fiori UI in SAP Business Application Studio

The Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model provided by SAP is a powerful, opinionated framework designed to streamline the process of building enterprise-grade services and applications that are scalable, extensible, and cloud-ready. In this extensive guide, we will delve deeply into each and every step required to build a full-fledged CAP project from scratch using SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), enhance it with a Fiori UI, and deploy the entire application stack to the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) on Cloud Foundry. This guide is structured with elaborate explanations for beginners and intermediate developers alike.

Prerequisites and Initial Setup

Before we begin, ensure you have a valid SAP BTP trial or productive account with appropriate entitlements, particularly for SAP HANA Cloud and SAP Build Work Zone, which are essential for running managed application routers and deploying HTML5 applications. You also need access to SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), which will serve as your cloud-based IDE for this project.

== 1. Creating a Dev Space in SAP Business Application Studio ==

  1. Open your browser and navigate to the SAP Business Application Studio.

  2. On the landing page, click on the "Create Dev Space" button, which will prompt you to select a template.

  3. From the available options, choose "Full-Stack Cloud Application" as it comes pre-configured with essential tools for CAP and Fiori development.

  4. Assign a recognizable name to your dev space, for example, cap-bookshop, to easily identify it later.

  5. Check the box labeled "SAP HANA Tools" to enable support for creating and managing HANA database artifacts.

  6. Click on "Create Dev Space" and wait patiently for the status of the space to change to "RUNNING."

  7. Once the dev space is up and running, click on it to launch the integrated development environment where you will build your application.

== 2. Provisioning a SAP HANA Cloud Instance ==

  1. Log in to the SAP BTP Cockpit using your credentials.

  2. Navigate into your global account and select the appropriate subaccount.

  3. Under the navigation pane, click on "Service Marketplace" to view the available services.

  4. Search for "SAP HANA Cloud" and click on it when it appears in the search results.

  5. Click on the "Create" button to begin provisioning a new HANA database instance.

  6. Choose the SAP HANA Database (ensure you do not select SAP HANA Cloud Tools).

  7. Sign-In with cloud foundry account and select your space from the dropdown
  8. Enter a unique name for the instance (e.g., hana), specify your required configurations, set an admin password.

  9. Administrator Section:

    • Administrator Username: Auto-filled as DBADMIN.

    • Administrator Password: Enter a secure password.

    • Confirm Password: Retype to confirm.
      🔐Keep in mind: Passwords for DBADMIN expire every 180 days, so set a reminder to change them before expiry.

  11. Configure SAP HANA Database Settings

    In this step, you'll define your database instance's technical specifications.

     

    • Available Memory Range:
      In the trial version, it's fixed at 16 GB.

    ℹ️Note: Trial accounts come with fixed configurations for memory and storage. For more flexibility, a commercial account is required.

  12. Network & Cloud Connector Configuration

    In this step, configure the network access and optionally enable the Cloud Connector if needed.

    Allow all IP addresses (Selected in this guide)
    Suitable for trial and development — easiest for testing external tools.
  13. Click "Create" in final stage

  14. The provisioning process may take a few minutes to complete. Once done, your database will be ready for binding.

  15. (Important) Whenever trying to deploy or bind your service or application ensure HANA Cloud is Started and running

== 3. Creating a New CAP Project Using Terminal Commands ==

  1. Inside your running BAS dev space, open a new terminal window by navigating to the menu: Terminal → New Terminal.

  2. Change the current directory to your desired workspace, typically located at /home/user/projects/:

cd /home/user/projects/

  1. Run the CAP project initialization command:

cds init bookshop
cd bookshop
 

  1. Your project will now have a structured directory layout containing essential folders such as db for data models, for services, app for UI modules (currently empty), and package.json which manages dependencies.

== 4. Modeling Data and Defining Services ==

4.1 Define the Data Model

Create a new CDS file in the db folder, typically named data-model.cds:

namespace my.bookshop;

entity Books {
  key ID    : Integer;
      title : String;
      author: String;
      stock : Integer;
}

4.2 Add Sample Data in CSV Format

Create a new CSV file inside the db/data directory named my.bookshop-Books.csv:

ID;title;author;stock
1;The Hobbit;J.R.R. Tolkien;15
2;Harry Potter;J.K. Rowling;30
3;The Alchemist;Paulo Coelho;20

4.3 Define a CAP Service

Inside the srv folder, create a service definition file named catalog-service.cds:

using my.bookshop from '../db/data-model';

service CatalogService {
  entity Books as projection on my.bookshop.Books;
}

== 5. Configuring for SAP HANA Deployment ==

CAP defaults to SQLite for local development, but to deploy on SAP BTP, you must configure it to use SAP HANA.

  1. Add HANA configuration for production:

cds add hana --for production

  1. Add hybrid setup (for both dev and prod compatibility):

cds add hana --for hybrid

  1. Generate a Multi-Target Application (MTA) descriptor:

cds add mta

  1. Install all necessary project dependencies:

npm install

== 6. Previewing Your CAP App Locally ==

To see how your app behaves locally, start the development server:

cds watch

The terminal will display a URL where your CAP service is accessible. Click the link to open a preview interface, which features a basic UI generated using Fiori Elements.

== 7. Adding a Managed Application Router ==

  1. Right-click on the mta.yaml file and select Create MTA Module from Template.

  2. Choose the Approuter Configuration template.

  3. Follow the wizard:

    • Use a Managed Approuter.

    • Name it samplerouter or another descriptive identifier.

    • Indicate that a Fiori UI will be added later.

  4. Finish the wizard to generate the approuter configuration, including a xs-security.json file.

== 8. Adding XSUAA for Authentication and Authorization ==

XSUAA is required for deploying CAP apps to Cloud Foundry with authentication support.

cds add xsuaa --for production
npm install

This updates your package.json and adds authentication requirements under the "requires" section.

== 9. Creating a SAP Fiori UI App ==

  1. Right-click mta.yaml → Create MTA Module from Template.

  2. Choose SAP Fiori Application and select List Report Page as the template.

  3. In the Data Source step:

    • Select Local CAP Project

    • Pick the bookshop project

    • Choose the CatalogService (Node.js)

  4. Confirm Books as the main entity.

  5. Set module name (e.g., booksui), title, and namespace.

  6. Enable deployment options and FLP configuration.

  7. For deployment:

  8. Target: Cloud Foundry

  9.  

    • Destination: Local CAP Project API

    • Semantic Object: bookshop

    • Action: show

    • Title: Books

Finish the wizard to generate the UI module.

== 10. Deploying the Application to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry ==

10.1 Login to Cloud Foundry

Use the command palette:

CTRL + Shift + P → CF: Login to Cloud Foundry

Provide the API endpoint, credentials, and select the correct organization and space.

10.2 Bind the HANA HDI Container

  1. Open the SAP HANA Projects panel in BAS.

          Bind to a HDI Container

         Select "+ Create a new service instance" 

  2. Click bookshop-db → Bind → Create new service instance.

  3. Name it bookshop-db and enable auto undeploy.

10.3 Build and Deploy the MTA Archive

 

Run the following in your terminal:

mbt build
cf deploy mta_archives/bookshop_1.0.0.mtar

This creates and deploys your application archive.

== 11. Verifying Deployment and Launching Your Fiori App ==

  1. In the BTP cockpit, go to Services → Instances. You should see instances for the db, approuter, CAP service, and xsuaa.

  2. Go to HTML5 Applications (enabled via SAP Build Work Zone) and find your booksui app.

  3. Launch the app to see the same Fiori UI you previewed locally, now hosted on the cloud.

entry.title: Semantic Querying with SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph using RDF, SPARQL, and Generative AI in Python
entry.content:
SAP Knowledge Graph is now generally available (Q1 2025) and is poised to fundamentally change how data relationships are mapped and queried. With grounded intelligence, knowledge graphs are crucial in enabling AI agents for reasoning and retrieval with context and high accuracy. SAP Knowledge Graph Engine includes a native RDF triplestore and supports SQL/SPARQL interoperability, allowing both semantic and relational access. 

A few key concepts when working with SAP Knowledge Graph:
 
Resource Description Framework (RDF): Graph-like format utilizing the relationship between entities to connect information

RDF Triples

  1. Subject - The entity being described (e.g., a product, person, or organization).
  2. Predicate - The relationship between the subject and the object (e.g., "manufactures," "owns," "located in").
  3. Object - The value or entity linked to the subject (e.g., a location, another person, or an attribute).

RDF enables storing and querying relationships dynamically, making it a key foundation for knowledge graphs and AI-driven reasoning. 

URI (Uniform Resource Identifier): Unique identifier for an entity or relationship within the knowledge graph.

This blog covers an integration of SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph with Gen AI Hub on Python, uploading and querying an RDF graph. For starters, refer to the SAP Discovery Center Mission –  Building Intelligent Data Applications with SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graphs.

 

Prerequisites

  • Access to SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine
  • Access to SAP Generative AI Hub SDK (SAP AI Core on SAP BTP)
  • A Python environment

Step 1: Set up an SAP HANA Cloud instance where Triple Store is enabled

Within SAP HANA Database, enable ‘Triple Store’ on SAP HANA Cloud Central tool during instance provisioning

  • Step 5: Advanced Setting tab
  • Prerequisite: 3 vCPUs compute / 48 GB memory or more
  • Recommended: At least 8 vCPUs & more for enhanced performance

The following steps uses Python programmatically.

Step 2: Create Knowledge Graphs (RDF format)

We start by loading RDF/XML file into an RDFLib graph. RDFLib is a Python library that supports the creation and manipulation of RDF triples programmatically. The RDF file used in this example refers to the open-source Financial Plan Statements – Cash Flow. The raw data contains many predicates, but for this use case, we will filter triples containing specific predicates like rowID, dscrpt (description), and infl_outfl (inflow/outflow).

Define namespaces matching the RDF predicates. Create a new graph to store filtered triples that contain only relevant predicates. This reduces data noise and keeps the RDF data focused on important attributes.

Finally, the filtered data is saved in .ttl format for ingestion into SAP HANA.

from rdflib import Graph, Namespace

g = Graph()
g.parse("rows.rdf", format="xml")

ns1 = Namespace("http://www.socrata.com/rdf/terms#")
ns2 = Namespace("https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/_4ubz-8kkx/")
filtered = Graph()
filtered.bind("ns1", ns1)
filtered.bind("ns2", ns2)

for s, p, o in g:
    if p in (ns1.rowID, ns2.dscrpt, ns2.infl_outfl):
        filtered.add((s, p, o))
filtered.serialize(destination="rows_filtered_100.ttl", format="turtle")

Step 3: Loading RDF Graph into SAP HANA Cloud

Using SAP HANA’s dbapi, the filtered RDF data is uploaded into a named RDF graph within SAP HANA Cloud via a stored procedure (SPARQL_EXECUTE). This makes the semantic data available for SPARQL querying within the database.

This integration enables leveraging SAP HANA’s graph engine and SPARQL query capabilities on the ingested RDF.

from hdbcli import dbapi

conn = dbapi.connect(user="USERNAME", password="PASSWORD", address="ADDRESS", port=443)
cursor = conn.cursor()

rdf_filename = "rows_filtered_100.ttl"
graphname = 'financial_plan_statements_cashflow_filtered_100'

with open(rdf_filename, 'r') as rdf_file:
    rdf_data = rdf_file.read()

request_hdrs = (
    'rqx-load-protocol: true\r\n'
    f'rqx-load-filename: {rdf_filename}\r\n'
    f'rqx-load-graphname: {graphname}\r\n'
)

cursor.callproc('SPARQL_EXECUTE', (rdf_data, request_hdrs, '', None))
cursor.commit()

Once ready, the filtered RDF graph is loaded into SAP HANA Cloud’s graph store. You can view the triples that you’ve uploaded via the HANA Cloud Central SQL Console. The SQL Console is configured to show a maximum number of 250,000 rows, configurable via settings.

 

Note: Under Instances, copy SQL Endpoint (top right) to use for HANA Cloud API connectivity.

Step 4: Setting Up Generative AI Hub SDK

The Generative AI Hub SDK provides an interface to LLMs.

To bridge natural language questions to RDF data queries, we configure an SAP AI Core client and proxy to use an LLM. In this case, anthropic—claude-3.5-sonnet is used. Do note you’ll have to deploy the model you’re using, and you can do so via SAP AI Launchpad.

from ai_core_sdk.ai_core_v2_client import AICoreV2Client
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.amazon import ChatBedrock
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.core.proxy_clients import get_proxy_client

def setup(): 
    proxy_client = get_proxy_client('gen-ai-hub')
    anthropic = ChatBedrock(
        model_name="anthropic--claude-3.5-sonnet",
        proxy_client=proxy_client
    )

    # Connect to SAP HANA Cloud
    conn = dbapi.connect(
        user='',
        password='',  # alternatively, set up os.environ 
        address='',
        port=443,
    )

    return anthropic, conn

Step 5: Extracting Triples from RDF

This retrieves RDF triples. The results are parsed from XML into Python data structures. These triples represent graph data, not traditional database tables or columns.

def extract_schema(question: str, conn) -> list[dict]:
    sparql = """
    SELECT ?s ?p ?o WHERE {
        ?s ?p ?o.
        FILTER(STRSTARTS(STR(?s), "https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/_4ubz-8kkx"))
    }
    """
    # Execute SPARQL query via stored procedure
    # Parse response XML into triple dicts (subject, predicate, object)
    # Return list of triples as dictionaries or empty list if none found

Step 6: Analyzing Triples with AI and Generating Natural Language Responses

The extracted triples, combined with the user’s natural language question, are fed into Anthropic Claude (via Gen AI Hub SDK and LangChain integrations) to generate a concise and coherent natural language answer. 

def generate_response_structured(question: str, results: pd.DataFrame, anthropic) -> str:
    if results.empty:
        return "No results found for your query."
    
    prompt_template = """Convert the following query results into a natural language response to the user's question. 
    Keep the response concise but informative. Include relevant numbers and comparisons where appropriate.
    
    Question: {question}
    
    Results:
    {results}
    
    Response:
    """
    
    prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(prompt_template).invoke({
        "question": question,
        "results": results.to_string()
    })
    
    response = anthropic.invoke(prompt)
    return response.content

The output, adjusted to show only natural language: 

This end-to-end system enables users to ask complex questions over RDF graphs stored in SAP HANA Cloud — all with natural language.

This pipeline showcases a powerful synergy of

  • Semantic data managed with RDF and SPARQL
  • Enterprise-grade persistence and querying on SAP HANA Cloud
  • Generative AI that interprets user intent and translates it into precise data queries
  • Natural language explanations that make complex data accessible

AI-assisted analytics and querying on complex RDF datasets is suitable for financial statements, geographical data, and more—delivering real-time insights.

Leave any comments below!

entry.title: Fast-Track Your AI Journey with SAP HANA Cloud’s Vector Engine Through Our New Expert Guided service
entry.content:
Fast-Track Your AI Journey with Hands-On Experience of SAP HANA Cloud’s Vector Engine Through Our New Expert-Guided Implementation service

Are you ready to put generative AI to work using your own enterprise data - in a secure and scalable way?

SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council is piloting a new expert guided implementation service (EGI) focused on multi-model programming with SAP HANA Cloud vector engine and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). As a participant, you’ll gain exclusive early access to content, influence the final offering with your feedback, and build hands-on experience before general availability. This isn’t just another webinar - it’s a practical, step-by-step experience where you’ll configure, test, and build solutions in your own environment, with guidance from an expert throughout.

This structured approach ensures you gain both theoretical knowledge and practical experience, helping you build confidence in using SAP solutions effectively. Since SAP Enterprise Support is included with every SAP cloud subscription, you can access this offering at no additional cost. 

After the completion of the 5 day remote workshop, you will have the knowledge of multiple model programming, including scenarios using spatial and graph processing, AutoML, vector engine and document store and the knowledge to query a document store through generative AI.


What You’ll Gain
 This service will equip you with the skills to leverage:

  • Spatial and graph data processing
  • AutoML and document stores
  • RAG application that connects enterprise data to LLMs
  • Vector search in SAP HANA Cloud

Why It Matters?
With the vector engine, SAP HANA Cloud becomes the bedrock of enterprise-grade AI applications - supporting similarity search, contextual insights, and intelligent applications all from within your secure SAP landscape.

What You’ll Do?

  • Set up and develop in a HANA Cloud trial or productive account
  • Work directly with spatial, graph, and vector data models
  • Extend a real RAG scenario using your own development environment
  • Gain practical experience with SAP expert support

Who Should Join?
The service is tailor-made for:

  • Developers and Data Scientists building intelligent apps
  • Process consultants driving innovation
  • Modelers exploring new use cases in SAP HANA Cloud

Join the EGI service and gain first-hand experience with SAP’s latest innovation in multi-model programming. Learn more and register for the service by contacting SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council through esac@sap.com

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 

About the author: 

  • Robert Kelly is Architect Lead for BTP for SAP Enterprise Support. Based in Dublin, Ireland, Robert has been with SAP since 2005, holding various roles across the organization. Today, he focuses on helping businesses implement their digital transformation through SAP Enterprise Support offerings.

entry.title: Handling CSV Data Deployment in CAP HDI Without Data Loss
entry.content:
Handling CSV Data Deployment in CAP HDI Without Data Loss

Introduction

In SAP CAP, deploying CSV data using .hdbtabledata files means the HDI container assumes full ownership of the data. As a result, any changes to the CSV file will overwrite the table content during the next deployment.

Background

Have you ever encountered a scenario where updating CSV data led to unexpected data loss? Here’s our experience:

In our hdi-content module, we maintained a UOM.csv (Unit of Measure) file, which is preloaded into the UOM table. This ensures that customers subscribing to our SaaS solution, SAP Omni Channel Promotional Pricing (OPPS), have immediate access to standard UOMs for defining product promotions. Since the UOM table is also exposed via CAP CDS views in the Fiori UI, customers can add their own entries as well.

The Issue

While working on a new feature that introduced another table with pre-delivered data, we discovered that any modification to the CSV file—such as adding or updating UOMs—could result in the loss of customer-maintained data in the UOM table. With many customers using our SaaS solution, this posed a significant risk of accidental data loss whenever the UOM.csv content was changed.

We needed a reliable solution to prevent customer data loss during deployments.

Exploring CAP Recommendations

According to the CAP documentation, you can use the include_filter option in .hdbtabledata to restrict which records are affected. This requires an additional column to distinguish between CSV data and user-generated data. In our case, we already had an origin column:

  • origin = '02': CSV data
  • origin = '10': Customer-created entries

However, even with include_filter, we found that customer data was still being lost, and only the CSV content remained after deployment.

The CAP troubleshooting guide references SAP Note 2922271, but its solution is tailored for @SAP/hdi-deploy and not directly applicable to @SAP/hdi-dynamic-deploy.

The Solution

With support from the HANA-DB-DI team, we identified a practical approach to address this challenge. We hope sharing this helps others building multitenant applications on CAP and HANA Cloud.

Deployment 1: Undeploy .hdbtabledata Without Deleting Data

First, set the HDI deployer's environment variable HDI_DEPLOY_OPTIONS in your MTA module properties to ensure the .hdbtabledata file is undeployed without deleting any data from the target table. Use the following configuration:

{
  "undeploy": [
    "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata"
  ],
  "path-parameter": {
    "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata:skip_data_deletion": "true"
  }
}

Then, proceed with the deployment and database upgrade. The logs below show this process:

2025-06-03T20:20:48.69+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [9d2219cebfa570a2]: Undeploying "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata"...
2025-06-03T20:20:48.69+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [9d2219cebfa570a2]: Undeploying "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata"... ok (0s 0ms)
2025-06-03T20:20:48.69+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [9d2219cebfa570a2]: Deploying "src/gen/OPPC.UOM.hdbtable$OPPC_UOM.validate"...
2025-06-03T20:20:48.69+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [9d2219cebfa570a2]: Expanded from "src/gen/OPPC.UOM.hdbtable"
2025-06-03T20:20:48.69+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [9d2219cebfa570a2]: Undeploying "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata$0.expand"...
2025-06-03T20:20:48.69+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [9d2219cebfa570a2]: Expanded from "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata"
2025-06-03T20:20:48.69+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [9d2219cebfa570a2]: The "skip_data_deletion" make parameter is set; no data will be deleted from the target table "OPPC_UOM"
2025-06-03T20:20:48.69+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [9d2219cebfa570a2]: Deploying "src/gen/OPPC.UOM.hdbtable$OPPC_UOM.validate"... ok (0s 11ms)
2025-06-03T20:20:48.69+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [9d2219cebfa570a2]: Undeploying "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata$0.expand"... ok (0s 12ms)

Deployment 2: Deploy .hdbtabledata with include_filter

  1. Remove the HDI deployer's HDI_DEPLOY_OPTIONS environment variable used in the previous deployment.
  2. Update the .hdbtabledata file to include the include_filter option.
  3. Deploy and upgrade the database.

However, this deployment failed because the include_filter matched existing records in the table, as shown in the logs:

2025-06-03T20:31:52.57+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] ERR Error: com.sap.hana.di.tabledata: The "include_filter" definitions match with 4364 records that already exist in the "OPPC_UOM" table [8251521]
2025-06-03T20:31:52.57+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] ERR Error: com.sap.hana.di.tabledata: Deploying "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata$0.expand"... failed [8212145]
2025-06-03T20:31:52.57+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] ERR Error: Worker 1 has encountered an error; all remaining jobs will be canceled [8214600]
2025-06-03T20:31:52.57+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] ERR Error: Processing work list... failed [8212102]
2025-06-03T20:31:52.57+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] ERR Error: Making... failed [8211605]
2025-06-03T20:31:52.57+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] ERR Error: Starting make in the container "679623F6772E4C3BA111AB4C1775754E" with 1 files to deploy, 0 files to undeploy... failed [8214168]

This deployment would only succeed if the database entries matching the CSV content were removed beforehand. In a multitenant application with many productive subscriptions, manually removing data for all tenants is not practical, and we lacked an automated solution for this process.

Possible Approaches

  • Automated Cleanup: If the origin values are consistently determined by application logic, you could develop an automated task to clean up these records. This cleanup would need to be performed as a separate deployment step before deploying the .hdbtabledata file, though this approach requires additional effort.
  • Alternative Approach: Instead, you can maintain new CSV content with a different origin value (e.g., origin='03'). This way, only the new CSV data is redeployed, and all existing data in the database table remains unchanged. Adjust the include_filter to target origin='03'.

The following logs show a successful deployment using this approach:

2025-06-03T20:36:18.52+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [be82fcfafc2dc1ee]: "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata"
2025-06-03T20:36:18.52+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [be82fcfafc2dc1ee]: ]
.
.
.
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Undeploying "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata$0.expand"...
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Expanded from "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata"
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Deploying "src/gen/OPPC.UOM.hdbtable$OPPC_UOM.validate"...
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Expanded from "src/gen/OPPC.UOM.hdbtable"
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Deploying "src/gen/OPPC.UOM.hdbtable$OPPC_UOM.validate"... ok (0s 7ms)
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Undeploying "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata$0.expand"... ok (0s 15ms)
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Undeploying "src/csv/OPPC-UOM.csv"...
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Undeploying "src/csv/OPPC-UOM.csv"... ok (0s 5ms)
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Deploying "src/csv/OPPC-UOM.csv"...
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Deploying "src/csv/OPPC-UOM.csv"... ok (0s 3ms)
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Deploying "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata$0.expand"...
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Expanded from "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata"
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Inserted 1 records of the data source file "OPPC-UOM.csv" into the target table "OPPC_UOM"
2025-06-03T20:42:49.12+0530 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT [d9a3e16053ae1f81]: Deploying "src/gen/OPPC-UOM.hdbtabledata$0.expand"... ok (0s 43ms)

References

  • Deployment Options in HDI
  • @sap/hdi-dynamic-deploy
  • CAP Troubleshooting: How do I keep existing data?
  • https://sap.stackenterprise.co/questions/73269 

Special Thanks

Special thanks to the HANA-DB-DI team members, particularly Pavel Sluzkin and Alexander Bunte, for their invaluable support and guidance!

entry.title: Supporting the Cloud-Journey: A New Chapter for our SAP HANA Certification
entry.content:
As part of our SAP Certification for Operations Partners program, we offer a range of certifications that recognize partners delivering high-quality services for SAP® solutions and supporting customers on their digital transformation journeys. To reflect the growing importance of cloud technologies, we’ve updated our SAP HANA operations certification, now titled “SAP HANA Cloud and database operations.”

The cloud is no longer a destination but a continuous journey of optimization and innovation. To help our partners confidently lead their customers on this path, we’ve enhanced the certification to focus on the practical, day-to-day realities of SAP HANA Cloud deployments.

The updated certification places stronger emphasis on cloud-related content and highlights the core competencies required for:

  • Seamless migrations to SAP HANA Cloud
  • Operational management of SAP HANA Cloud instances, including performance monitoring and security

By demonstrating these skills, partners reinforce their role in confidently guiding customers through every phase of their cloud journey.

With its expanded scope, the certification now reflects a partner’s end-to-end expertise across the SAP HANA Cloud lifecycle - serving as a strong differentiator in delivering sustained value in the cloud.

Existing certified partners will benefit from these enhancements at no additional cost and will complete the extended content during their next re-certification audit.

 

SAP Certifications for Operations Partners - overview of certifications

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Information on the certification program for operations partners and an overview of all certified partners can be found in the SAP Operations Partner Guide.

entry.title: Building a Compelling Business Case to Move Your Database to the Cloud
entry.content:
Migrating to SAP HANA Cloud can be a game-changer for your business, offering unparalleled performance, scalability, and cost efficiency. By building a strong business case, you can clearly articulate the benefits and ROI to stakeholders, making it easier to gain their support and approval. Embrace the future of data management and take your business to the next level with SAP HANA Cloud. This blog will guide you through building a compelling business case to move your database to SAP HANA Cloud. 

To start, focus on an analysis of your current environment.  Describe your on-premise data infrastructure, including hardware, software, and maintenance requirements as well as the costs associated with maintenance, licensing, and IT staff.  Also, highlight any challenges you are currently facing, such as limited scalability, performance issues, or high downtime.

Now, it is time to detail out the benefits of moving to SAP HANA Cloud.  We can help!  Together with IDC, we have developed an online tool - a short assessment to help you communicate the potential value of using HANA Cloud.

The assessment is just 6 questions and will generate a personalized report to include potential cost savings, performance and productivity improvements, business enablement benefits, and return on investment.

Next, conduct a comprehensive TCO comparison between your current on-premises setup and the proposed HANA Cloud solution. Make sure to factor in both the upfront and ongoing costs for each scenario.  Based on the projected cost savings and business benefits, estimate the expected ROI of the cloud migration (the personalized report will help you here). This helps stakeholders understand the long-term value of the proposed project.

Finally, think further about your migration strategy, including any tools and services you'll use to simplify the process.  We can also assist you here!  Learn about a wide a range of migration automation tools, accelerators, and services to streamline the complete migration journey to SAP HANA Cloud - from initial planning to go-live, including a free migration assessment to help you design an optimal architecture and deployment strategy.  

At this point, you will also want to address potential risks, like data security concerns or migration downtime, and propose strategies to mitigate them. This shows that you've thoroughly considered all aspects of the migration. Then, summarize your findings and make a clear, data-driven recommendation on whether to proceed with the cloud migration.  Outline potential the next steps for the migration project, including planning, proof of concept (PoC), execution, and ongoing monitoring.

Good luck in taking the first step towards database modernization!

Learn more from IDC’s business value study on SAP HANA Cloud or visit the SAP HANA modernization web page.

entry.title: Grandes players do Rio implementando SAP S/4HANA com abordagem Greenfield
entry.content:
No coração do Rio de Janeiro, grandes empresas estão adotando transformações disruptivas com SAP S/4HANA, escolhendo o caminho do Greenfield – construindo do zero e criando processos operacionais do futuro.

E para você que deseja entrar no mercado de projetos SAP, você deve saber como é um projeto de implementação “green”. 

✅ Por que escolher o Greenfield?

* Processos limpos e otimizados (Reinício completo sem depender de customizações antigas);
* Adoção de melhores práticas SAP (Construção nova permite aproveitar as funcionalidades nativas do S/4HANA e SAP BTP);
* Convergência digital (Integração ativa com nuvem e automação para acelerar inovação.

Com isso que escrevi, hoje você se sente apto para seguir num projeto greenfield? Escreva nos comentários.

#SAP

entry.title: Build and Deploy a Full-Stack SAP CAPM App with Fiori UI on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry
entry.content:
Build and Deploy a Full-Stack SAP CAP Application with Fiori UI on BTP Cloud Foundry – A Complete Guide.

The Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model by SAP is a robust, opinionated framework that simplifies the creation of enterprise-grade services and applications that are scalable, extensible, and cloud-ready. In this in-depth guide, we walk you through every step of building a full-stack CAP project from the ground up using SAP Business Application Studio (BAS). You'll enhance it with a Fiori UI and deploy the entire application stack to SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) on Cloud Foundry. This guide is crafted with detailed, beginner-friendly explanations and valuable insights for intermediate developers aiming to master modern SAP application development.

 ==Prerequisites and Initial Setup==

 Before we begin, ensure you have an SAP BTP trial or productive account with the necessary entitlements, including SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition), and SAP Business Application Studio (BAS). These are essential for developing, deploying, and running CAP applications with Fiori UI on Cloud Foundry.

=== Creating a SAP HANA Cloud Instance===

To persist and manage data in your CAP application, you need to provision a SAP HANA Cloud instance in your BTP subaccount.

1.Log in to the SAP BTP Cockpit using your credentials.

2.Navigate into your global account and select the appropriate subaccount.

3.Under the navigation pane, click on "Service Marketplace" to view the available services.

4.Search for "SAP HANA Cloud" and click on it when it appears in the search results.

5.Click on the "Create" button to begin provisioning a new HANA database instance.

 

  1. Accessing SAP HANA Cloud Central
  2. Create New SAP HANA Cloud Instance

 

8.Sign-In with cloud foundry account and select your space from the dropdown

9.🔐 Administrator Section

During SAP HANA Cloud instance creation, you’ll need to configure administrative access:

  • Administrator Username: Pre-filled as DBADMIN (not editable)
  • Administrator Password: Enter a strong, secure password
  • Confirm Password: Re-enter the password to confirm

 

 

🔒Note: The DBADMIN password expires every 180 days. Be sure to set a reminder to update it before expiration to avoid access issues

 

10.Configure SAP HANA Database Settings

  • Available Memory Range: In the trial version, it's fixed at 16 GB.

11.SAP HANA Database Advanced Setting

🌐Network & Connectivity Settings

  • Allow All IP Addresses
  • Enable this option to allow unrestricted access to the database from any IP address.

🔗Enable Cloud Connector (Optional)

If your application or data sources require secure access to on-premise systems via the SAP Cloud Connector:

  • Toggle Enable Cloud Connector to ON
  • This allows secure integration between your SAP HANA Cloud instance and on-premise environments

 

12.Final Review and Create Instance

After configuring all the necessary settings — including instance name, memory size, admin credentials, network access, and optional Cloud Connector — take a moment to carefully:

 

  • Review the Administrator Username and Password
  • Verify Connectivity Settings (e.g., Allow All IP Addresses)
  • Confirm the Cloud Connector status (enabled or disabled)
  • Ensure there are no missing or incorrect entries

Once everything looks correct, click Create to start provisioning your SAP HANA Cloud instance.

 

⏳The process may take a few minutes. After successful creation, the status will change to Running — your database is now ready for use!

 

 

  1. Steps to Manage Configuration and Instance Mapping in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Click “Manage Configuration”:
  • Go to “Instance Mapping”:
  • Provide Cloud Foundry Environment Details:
  • Set the Environment to: Cloud Foundry
  • Click review and save

 

== 1. Creating a Dev Space in SAP Business Application Studio ==

  1. Open your browser and navigate to the SAP Business Application Studio.
  2. On the landing page, click on the "Create Dev Space" button, which will prompt you to select a template.
  3. From the available options, choose "Full-Stack Cloud Application" as it comes pre-configured with essential tools for CAP and Fiori development.
  4. Assign a recognizable name to your dev space, for example, capm-book-app, to easily identify it later.
  5. Check the box labeled "SAP HANA Tools" to enable support for creating and managing HANA database artifacts.
  6. Click on "Create Dev Space" and wait patiently for the status of the space to change to "RUNNING."
  7. Once the dev space is up and running, click on it to launch the integrated development environment where you will build your application.

 

2.Step-by-Step: Creating a New CAP Project from Template

  • Open SAP Business Application Studio (BAS)
  • Log in to your trial or productive BAS workspace.

2.1 Open the Command Palette

  • Click on the View menu → Find Command
  • Or press F1 or Ctrl + Shift + P to open the Command Palette

2.3 Choose   New   Project   from Template

  • From the list, choose:  "CAP Project" (Cloud Application Programming Model)
  • Click Next

2.3 Fill Project Details

  • Project Name: e.g., bookshop
  • Choose Runtime: Node.js
  • Choose  Add  Samples   Yes (optional, useful for beginners)
  • Click  Finish

 

 

2.4 . Open in Workspace

  • If prompted: Click "Open in New Workspace"
  • The wizard will scaffold a full CAP project structure with:

== Modeling Data and Defining Services ==

  1. Define the Data Model

Create a new CDS file in the db folder, typically named data-model.cds:

 3.1 Add Sample Data in CSV Format

Create a new CSV file inside the db/data directory named my.bookshop-Books.csv:

   ID,title,stock,author

  1,The Pragmatic Programmer,42,Andrew Hunt

  2,Clean Code,37,Robert C. Martin

  3,Domain-Driven Design,15,Eric Evans

 4,Introduction to Algorithms,21,Thomas H. Cormen

 5,Design Patterns,18,Erich Gamma

 6,Refactoring,25,Martin Fowler

7,The Clean Coder,30,Robert C. Martin

8,Head First Design Patterns,40,Eric Freeman

9,Working Effectively with Legacy Code,10,Michael Feathers

10,Continuous Delivery,12,Jez Humble

                                                                  

3.2 Define a CAP Service

Inside the  srv  folder, create a service definition file named catalog-service.cds:

3.3 @odata.draft.enabled – CAPM Annotation:

  • The @odata.draft.enabled annotation enables draft handling for an entity in SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model). It supports Fiori Elements UIs by allowing users to create, update, save drafts, and finalize records without writing custom CRUD logic.
  • Adds special fields: IsActiveEntity, HasDraftEntity, HasActiveEntity.
  •  

  ⚠️ Notes:

·       Only works with OData V4.

 

 

3.4 Steps to Add a Fiori App Using SAP Fiori Generator:

  • Click File → New Project from Template
  • Select “SAP Fiori Application” and click Start
  •  Select “SAP Fiori Elements”

3.5 selecting the List Report Page template is a great choice for generating a powerful UI automatically from your CAP service.

  • The List Report Page is part of Fiori Elements, used to create a master-detail UI where

 

3.6 Choose Data Source and service selection :

  • If your CAP project is open in the same workspace
  • Use a local CAP project
  • Select Main Service Entity

 

3.7 After UI setup is finished:

Install dependencies
In your project root directory, run:

  •   npm install

 3.8 Start the CAP server with watch mode
Run:

  •     cds watch
  •   Starts your CAP service locally
  •  Automatically reloads when you make changes to .Cds, .js, or .json files

== 4. Configuring for SAP HANA and cloud foundary Deployment ==

CAP defaults to SQLite for local development, but to deploy on SAP BTP, you must configure it to use SAP HANA.

4.1  Add HANA configuration for production:

4.2  Adds XSUAA (SAP Authorization and Trust Management) to your project

  • Adding oauth2-configuration to xs-security.json for Local & CF Integration
  • Purpose Defines allowed callback URLs for authentication Allows redirect after deployment to Cloud Foundry (US10 region).

4.3 Add HTML5 Repo to Your Project:

  1. Adds configuration to support deploying UI modules (React, Fiori

  1. Updates the mta.yaml file to include:
  • html5-repo-host
  • html5-repo-runtime

 Enables deployment to SAP Launchpad / Work Zone or directly via managed app router

4.4 Adds an App Router to your CAP project for production deployment on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry.

1. The Application Router (Approuter) is a Node.js-based component that.

2.Routes requests to your CAP services (srv) and HTML5 apps (app).

3.Once deployed, the App Router becomes the single entry point URL for your entire application.

4.5 Adds mta.yaml file to your CAP project to enable deployment to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry using the Multi-Target Application (MTA) approach.

  • Creates the deployment descriptor required by Cloud Foundry
  • Combines your DB, service, UI, XSUAA, and HTML5 repo into one .mtar file
  • Manages Dependencies Links services like HANA, XSUAA, Approuter, and HTML5 apps together in one file.

  • Modules — what your app is made of
  • These are the individual deployable components (e.g., backend, UI, approuter).
  • Resources — external services your app uses
  • These are external backing services or dependencies your modules need.

4.6 Add workzone-standard  Launch your CAP app via a tile on SAP Work Zone standard edition

  • Adds a Work Zone module to your mta.yaml
  • This prepares the app for deployment to Work Zone.
  • Generates a manifest.json for Work Zone
  • Used to register the application (title, URL, etc.).

 

4.7 Install all necessary project dependencies:

4.8 Add Multi-Target Application Builder It comes from the SAP MTA SDK and builds all modules and combines them into a single .mtar file.

4.9 Deploy MTAR Archive to Cloud Foundry

  • The "Build and Deploy Completed" pop-up message appears after you build and deploy your CAPM or MTA project using SAP Business Application Studio or the CLI.

4.10 After deploying your MTA (.mtar) to Cloud Foundry, the application URL(s) become available in the Cloud Foundry space where you deployed.

 

 

Navigate to Your Destination in BTP Cockpit

  • View Your Configured Destinations
  • Test the destination (click on it → press "Check Connection")
  • Edit credentials, token URL, client ID/secret

4.11 After deploying an HTML5 application (like a Fiori or React app) to SAP BTP (Cloud Foundry) using html5-repo, the application URL becomes available
